div.h2: Memory Layout: Big-Endian vs Little-Endian — 64-bit Length Field
div.p: Value: 0x0000000000000018 (decimal 24 = bit-length of "abc") · Location: padded block bytes [56..63]

buf[0] = (val >> 56) & 0xFF = 0x00 ← Most significant buf[1] = (val >> 48) & 0xFF = 0x00 buf[2] = (val >> 40) & 0xFF = 0x00 buf[3] = (val >> 32) & 0xFF = 0x00 buf[4] = (val >> 24) & 0xFF = 0x00 buf[5] = (val >> 16) & 0xFF = 0x00 buf[6] = (val >> 8) & 0xFF = 0x00 buf[7] = (val >> 0) & 0xFF = 0x18 ← Least significant
What memcpy(&val, buf, 8) writes on x86 Native uint64_t stored LSB-first
SHA-256 reads this as: 0x1800000000000000 = 1729382256910270464 bits ≠ 24 bits The hash diverges from every correct implementation — no warning, no error.

text.tspan: // write_be64: always correct on any CPU
// WRONG on x86 — DO NOT USE:
// write_be64: always correct on any CPU
// WRONG on x86 — DO NOT USE:

div.p: Wrong endianness = wrong length field → Compression sees message length as 0x1800000000000000 bits instead of 0x18 → Final H[0..7] is completely different → Digest diverges from all correct impls → Certificate fingerprints won't match → HMAC tokens fail verification → No compile error, no runtime error — silent cryptographic failure
